data.title: Verizon: Stable, Undervalued, And Offering High Yield
data.description:
Verizon (NYSE:VZ) is an American technology and communications behemoth offering a compelling 6.12% dividend yield. High-quality, mature business is still growing, suggesting it is a relatively safe choice for investors who favor stable dividend income.

With 20 years of consecutive dividend growth and 24 years of consecutive dividend payouts, while trading at a forward P/E of 9.4, nearly a 35% discount to peers, Verizon appears to be an attractive entry point for long-term investors. The communications giant is a great diversification option, as its yield offers a defensive approach that benefits investors during turbulent times. The broader market has rallied significantly since the April lows without a meaningful pullback, indicating it may be time for a breather.

I think Verizon is a Buy with a $49 price target, suggesting a 10% upside. I think the company will perform in line with the benchmark, but its 6% dividend yield will offset the extra risk. I think having a defensive play in the portfolio is a great way for a diversified approach—it may not grow at double digits, but during turbulent times it offers limited downside.

FQ2 2025 Supports the Bullish Stance

On July 21, Verizon reported strong FQ2 2025, supporting the bullish narrative. Total revenue for the quarter was $34.5 billion, indicating a strong 5.2% increase year-over-year. The company's adjusted EPS grew as well, with a $1.22 quarterly result, representing an increase from $1.15 reported the same quarter last year (6% growth).

Moreover, the company reported 4.1% year-over-year adjusted EBITDA growth with $12.8 billion from $12.3 billion last year. Verizon has indicated that free cash flow came in at $8.8 billion over the first 6 months of the year, compared to $8.5 billion a year ago, about a 3.5% increase. The company is no longer a double-digit growth story, but recent results highlight its maturity and stability.

The company also highlighted its wireless service segment increased by about 2.2% on a year-over-year basis, with a $20.9 billion. Verizon added 300,000 new customers across its mobility and broadband platforms, with a fourth consecutive quarter of Core Prepaid subscribers. Investors should be satisfied with the company's results, signaling it has surpassed 5 million subscribers for their Fixed Wireless Access—a development reassuring investors.

It was a strong quarterly performance for Verizon, supported by raised full-year guidance. The company expects bottom-line growth of 2% at the midpoint, with cash flow from operations to come in at $38 billion at the midpoint. The company continues to execute and assures investors that its fundamentals remain intact.

Undervalued Compared to Peers With Strong Profitability Metrics

I think Verizon presents an attractive opportunity for long-term investors, as they may benefit from both the company trading at undervalued levels and a stable dividend yield. I believe this duo setup is ideal for dividend investors who favor purchasing a high-yielding stock at an enticing price.

Verizon was previously a cheaper stock from a forward P/E perspective, but with recent results and promising guidance, it should be trading at a premium. Despite a forward P/E elevated compared to its recent years, it is about 35% lower than the peers' average at 14.58. This suggests that Verizon is undervalued and there is room for upside.

On a PEG GAAP (TTM) basis, the company is valued at 0.17 compared to the sector median of 0.45. The overall communication segment appears to be undervalued from a PEG perspective, but a nearly 62% lower ratio supports our bullish stance for Verizon.

The following valuation metrics also suggest that Verizon is well-positioned for upside:

Metrics Verizon Sector Median
EV/EBITDA (FWD) 7.14 8.63
EV/EBIT(FWD) 11.26 16.69
Price/Book (FWD) 1.75 2.04
Price/Cash Flow (FWD) 4.99 8.17

From a valuation standpoint, the communication behemoth appears undervalued versus the industry's average. EV/EBIT on a forward at 11.26, while the average company trades at 16.69, indicates larger undervaluation, while other metrics favor Verizon by 15% to 30%. I think this may represent one of the largest valuation disconnects, considering Verizon is a dominant name in the industry, has a mature and stable business model, and offers a substantial yield to its shareholders via quarterly dividend.

Heavy-leverage Business, but Lower Interest Rates Should Reduce Risks

On both EV/Sales TTM and FWD basis, at 2.62 and 2.60, respectively, the communication giant is valued higher than its competitors, but it is mainly driven due to its significant obligations at $174.74 billion. Verizon is a high-leverage business, which is concerning.

At this point, its total obligations are almost equal to its market capitalization of $186 billion, which offsets some of the bullish narrative and supports lower valuations.

Looking at Verizon's balance sheet, long-term debt is a significant risk and concern for its business. Over the past few years, the company has reduced about 15% of its long-term debt from $143 billion in 2023 to $124 billion as of the most recent report. However, this remains a substantial amount, and maintaining it through a high-interest environment may be challenging.

Verizon's income statement indicates that last year the company spent $6.6 billion in interest expense. This was an increase of almost 23% compared to 2023. It is concerning and slightly surprising, considering that Verizon paid down around $17 billion of its long-term debt throughout the period, but its interest expenses increased dramatically.

I think that Verizon will benefit significantly from a lower interest rate environment, as it will be able to refinance at more favorable conditions. Such a large amount paid in interest expense represents about 3.5% of the market cap annually ($6.6 billion versus $186 billion). Reducing its leverage could offer substantial upside for investors.

Another important thing to consider is that markets are forward-looking. Despite being a heavy-leverage business, if the company progresses, the market may start pricing it early. That is why I believe it is an attractive entry point to consider for long-term investors, realizing the high-interest rate environment won't last forever.

Despite Market-average Growth, Profitability Metrics Indicate Outperformance

Verizon is past its rapid growth phase—it is a slowly but steadily growing business that is highly predictable with a defensive approach. The communication company's revenue grew 2.05% year-over-year, slightly below the industry's average of 3.36%. Its EBITDA grew 4.05% over the past 12 months, while competitors recorded a 6.45% increase.

To offset, Verizon's EBIT growth rate is expected at 13.18%, compared to peers' nearly 18% lower growth rate of 11.19%. It is a mixed bag, mainly due to its liability-heavy balance sheet and different business stages versus the competition.

Remarkably, Verizon's bottom line grew significantly—over 61% in the past 12 months, nearly 3x higher than the industry's average. That is also one of the reasons why the company is trading at a lower price-to-earnings ratio than the average business in the landscape. Return on equity grew by a significant 52% compared to peers' 14%, roughly 4x higher.

The profitability metrics signal Verizon outperformance. For instance, gross profit margin on a TTM basis is 59.48% versus peers' at 53.71%—about a 10% advantage. Verizon operates at nearly 3x greater operating margin (TTM) at 13.28% compared to the industry's average at 4.52%. This contributes to a significant cash from operations (TTM) of $37.10 billion, versus only $275 million reported by peers.

Other profitability metrics also signal Verizon outperformance versus the sector median:

Metrics Verizon Sector Median
Return on Common Equity (TTM) 18.26% 6.25%
Return on Total Capital (TTM) 7.15% 4.41%
Return on Total Assets (TTM) 4.75% 2.07%
CapEx/Sales (TTM) 12.39% 3.35%

Despite its slower-than-market growth and heavy leveraged capital structure, Verizon appears appealing from a fundamental analysis perspective. I think a high dividend yield makes it more attractive for long-term investors.

Dividend History and High Yield Rate Support the Upside

The company's dividend history is phenomenal—Verizon has consistently grown and paid its dividends to shareholders for over 20 years. To put it in perspective, the average company in the landscape has an 11-year track record with only one year of dividend growth. Shareholders value Verizon's dividend history—and due to its stability, it could be trading at a higher premium.

The dividend yield itself is exceptional. The average company in the industry offers 3.48%, thus Verizon's over 6% dividend yield is compelling for investors. The growth rate of the yield is not impressive at 1.88% on a TTM basis, but to be fair, I don't think investors would appreciate a higher increase, given its heavily leveraged capital structure. As it improves, Verizon could potentially increase its already high dividend yield further.

The cash dividend payout ratio at 56.45% versus the industry's average of 29.46% indicates elevated risk to future growth and the continuation of dividend payments. However, I think management understands and prioritizes a shareholder-friendly dividend approach. I believe it values its dividend history and would assume its continuation is its priority. Despite its challenges and debt-heavy balance sheet, it is a highly profitable business that operates at higher than market-average margins, supporting a dividend security stance. Net income rose significantly from $11.6 billion in 2023 to $17.5 billion in 2024, regardless of higher interest expense.

I think that Verizon will continue being an impressive dividend company, cherishing its history and building fundamentals for continued success.

It is a mature business that is highly profitable with an attractive dividend yield. Considering its debt-heavy financial structure, it will take time for fundamentals to improve, but at its current valuations, it appears as an interesting opportunity to consider for long-term investors. Its defensive approach, given its stability and predictability, may be a great offset to a growth-heavy portfolio, offering diversification.

Key Risks and Concerns

There are risks and concerns related to Verizon that may significantly weigh on its stock price in the short term.

Being debt-heavy with a significant interest rate burden, it remains a key concern for the following years. Despite management's progress to pay down its debt, over $6 billion in interest expenses are a significant addition to debt payments. If the Fed decides on higher-for-longer, it may affect the company's expectation to grow its bottom line, which could result in multiple contractions.

Verizon is a well-known brand and a dominant company in the communications landscape. However, it faces strong competition from T-Mobile and cable operators, such as Comcast, which may threaten Verizon's margins. Competitive pricing pressures may affect profitability, stressing the stock price.

I think that the postpaid phone subscriber decline of 9,000 in Q2 is slightly concerning. If the decline persists, it may indicate that Verizon is losing market share to its main competitors, AT&T and T-Mobile. I think this is the segment to follow and monitor—if Verizon reports net addition next quarter, it would indicate this was a minor bump and the red flag would fade.

Management execution is another focus area for investors. Verizon has raised its guidance, but a miss would significantly impact investor sentiment. On the other hand, if management exceeds its forecasts, Verizon could see a significant upside.

Conclusion: Verizon Should Perform in Line With the Broader Market, but 6% Yield Makes It Attractive

I think the current undervaluation and high dividend yield represent an attractive entry point to consider building a position in Verizon for long-term investors who favor dividend stocks. Investors may benefit from portfolio diversification with a well-known, defensive name. Verizon is a high-quality, mature business that operates at strong margins and is highly profitable.

Its dividend history and the consecutive growth rate are phenomenal traits of the business. Its net income indicates that, despite a debt-heavy capital structure and high interest expenses, it is likely to maintain its shareholder-friendly, high-yield dividend strategy. The risk may be elevated, but I don't think investors should be concerned about whether they would pause dividend growth or payout.

I believe Verizon is a Buy with a $49 price target, suggesting about a 12% increase. It would be in line with the average performance of the broader market, but the upside comes from a high dividend yield. I think Verizon is a great company to consider for investors who favor diversification and its defensive approach. With markets rallying without a significant pullback since April, it may be due for a pause, and Verizon should be an alternative to consider for investors.

26 Wall Street analysts suggest a $48.74 price target over the next 12 months, projecting 11.13% upside. This is in line with my expectations, as the stock is currently undervalued compared to peers, and a high dividend yield suggests the company should be trading at a premium. Verizon is a highly profitable business that supports its defensive approach and continued dividend growth.

data.title: Leonardo: Long-Term Winner With Strong Prospects Ahead
data.description:
Back in December 2022, I rated Leonardo S.p.a (FINMF)(OTCPK:FINMY) a Strong Buy (Leonardo: Significantly Undervalued But Poised To Grow). My thesis was clear: inflationary pressures warned the investors about profit margins, and they likely oversold the company, making it a value bargain. Secondly, already 3 and a half years ago, the company secured a big slice of defense contracts both in Europe and the US, focusing on more nice products than competitors, making it a “safe” investment. And finally, the clever acquisitions made to boost the growth, put the company in a good spot to further grow in the future.

In the meantime, I also wrote two other articles (Leonardo: A Great Long-Term Pick and Leonardo: Double-Digit Growth Since My First 'Strong Buy,' But There's Still Room To Go), indicating how Leonardo was pivotal for the rearmament of Europe in a growing period of uncertainty. After 3 and a half years, the company, which I recommended at $8.10, has yielded +600%.

The thesis of this article is clear: the idea I had is still valid today. Leonardo, not only proved to be among the world’s most important defense companies but showcased great capabilities in foreseeing the future needs of Europe. Therefore, Leonardo is still a company where to invest.

ReArm Europe

First, one of the major growth drivers of Leonardo is and will be ReArm Europe, a strategic initiative carried out by the European Union, which has the objective of boosting EU defense capabilities by 2030, investing a total of €800 billion. This plan comes in response to the Russian aggression in Ukraine and basically serves as a deterrent against future Russian aggression on European soil. Furthermore, the EU needs to quickly boost production on armored vehicles, drones, and ammunition, which proved to be crucial in high intensity warfare in Ukraine.

It is reasonable to conclude that Leonardo will receive a substantial amount of these funds since it is one of the major European defense firms. Moreover, ReArm Europe will be primarily dedicated to EU and Ukraine-based industries. This limits the possibility of giving away investments to the US-based companies, while boosting European defense production.

Drones Production and Future of Warfare

Another lesson from Ukraine regards the use of drones. Cheap and easy-to-produce FPV (first-person view) drones proved to be very effective against expensive and hard-to-replace armored vehicles. Moreover, UAVs (unmanned aerial vehicles) demonstrated a good cost-effectiveness ratio, especially against costly air defense systems. These advances in warfare quickly affected industries in the last year, since many analysts recognized their usefulness in future warfare. Leonardo, has already positioned itself in the industry by announcing a joint venture with Baykar, a Turkish company leader in the production of unmanned systems.

According to the June 2025 press release,

the new company, LBA Systems, will be based in Italy

(Source: Leonardo Press Release)

and will be a key player in Europe. Furthermore, Leonardo already possesses capabilities and experience in cutting-edge electronic systems, thus providing added value to the products of the joint venture.

Iveco Defence Acquisition

As for ground warfare, Leonardo didn’t emerge as a major player. In this sense, Rheinmetall (OTCPK:RNMBF)(OTCPK:RNMBY) proved to have an advantage in Europe, since it is leading armored vehicle production. In my opinion, this should not be viewed as a problem for the competitiveness of Leonardo, since the company has always been more electronic-warfare/navy/air-force-oriented, following the needs of Italian defense.

However, Leonardo quickly caught up. Back in October 2024, it established a joint venture with Rheinmetall for European tank production. More recently, the company has announced the acquisition of Iveco Defence (Iveco Group: (OTCPK:IVCGF)) for €1.7 billion. The group will be able to satisfy the demand for basically all ground defense vehicles, a sector that is estimated to represent 40% of the European future spending in armaments. Also in this case, the expertise of Leonardo in electronic warfare could represent a good synergy with Iveco Defence.

This strategic acquisition marks a significant step in Leonardo’s plan to strengthen its role as a leading, fully integrated Original Equipment Manufacturer (OEM) in the land defence domain. The acquisition further enhances the Group’s comprehensive portfolio of solutions for defence and security, covering both tracked and wheeled platforms.

(Source: Leonardo Press Release)

Valuation

The new updated guidance underlines what was said. The new orders are €1.25-€1.75 billion higher than the previous guidance. This result is probably due to ReArm Europe and represents very good news for the company.

The group net debt is also good news: according to the new guidance, it is projected to reach €1.1 billion, a promising trend considering it was €3.0 billion in June 2024.

Finally, another interesting result is Leonardo's revenue, which recorded a +11.7% compared to the first six months of 2024, and showcases the company’s strength and continuous growth thanks to its effective policies.

As for the valuation, I think a good comparison can be made using EV/EBITDA multiples of the major European defense companies. Rheinmetall has a multiple of 39.41x, BAE Systems (OTCPK:BAESF)(BASEY) 14.40x, Thales (OTCPK:THLEF)(OTCPK:THLLY) 17.97x, Safran (OTCPK:SAFRF)(OTCPK:SAFRY)15.17x and Airbus (OTCPK:EADSF)(OTCPK:EADSY) 14.03x.

Leonardo has an EV/EBITDA multiple of 13.72x, which indicates that the company may be cheaper than the others. On the other hand, the current PE of 26.62 is currently higher than the historical average, indicating a possible overvaluation, but the forward P/E and PEG , respectively 20.33 and 1.47, may indicate that future growth can sustain current valuation.

By doing simple math on the multiples, considering the average EV/EBITDA of the sector, Leonardo could be valued around $65.50 and €48.30 for the stock traded in Milan.

Rating and Conclusion

In summary, Leonardo seems to be undervalued compared to the other European industries in the sector, indicating a possible undervaluation. On the other hand, there’s one thing that worries me about the current price, despite being undervalued on paper. The recent meeting between Trump and Putin may have signaled the investors a higher probability of ending the conflict in Ukraine. While this can be a good signal for the markets, it could have the opposite effect on the defense stocks. Although this remains only a hypothesis, it is a risk that the investors should be aware of given the current geopolitical conditions.

That being said, the stock is still a very good pick. The defense spending in Europe is far from over, even in the case of a possible ceasefire in Ukraine. Moreover, Leonardo has been able to position itself in future high-demand sectors. Therefore, considering all the growth opportunities this company can offer in the current landscape, I still consider it a buying opportunity.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: A Ranking Of Net-Lease REITs By Investment Spread For Q2 2025
data.description:
Introduction

In May, we published a ranking of investment-grade, net-lease REITs that are part of our coverage universe. The May report incorporated the Q1 operating performance of the respective REITs and, in this report, we incorporate Q2 performance information. Readers who periodically rebalance their exposure to particular net-lease REITs may find value in this review.

As with other asset classes, REIT valuation and profitability often move in cycles. For real estate assets, there is the space market cycle, which has to do with the underlying supply and demand for a particular type of space, and there is the capital market cycle, which has to do with the valuations at which REITs trade given the current macroeconomic environment. This ranking has to do with the latter.

Source: Ralp L. Block, Investing in REITs, 4th Edition, John Wiley & Sons, Inc., 2012, Hoboken, N.J.

Also in May, we published a bullish article on Global Net Lease, Inc. (GNL) and, on June 26th, S&P raised the issuer credit rating on GNL by one notch to BB+ and simultaneously raised the issue-level rating on their senior unsecured notes to BBB-. Notwithstanding the positive development, GNL still has not secured an issuer-level investment-grade credit rating from any of the three major credit rating agencies and is thus not included here. Another item we want to point out is that we anticipate publishing an article on First Industrial Realty Trust, Inc. (FR) in the near future. We have completed substantially all the work on First Industrial and are comfortable in our understanding of their business to include their stock in the list. First Industrial is an investment-grade net-lease REIT that focuses on industrial real estate assets.

Source: First Industrial 10-K 2024, Moody’s, Fitch

Investor Takeaway

Early in Q2, the tariff announcement by the White House introduced volatility to the financial markets that has subsequently waned in its effect. While many of the REITs covered in this article suggested there is still uncertainty looming over the broader economy, they have not seen material disruptions to their own or their tenants' businesses. Agree Realty Corporation (ADC), Essential Properties Realty Trust, Inc. (EPRT), NNN REIT, Inc. (NNN), Realty Income Corporation (O), and W. P. Carey Inc. (WPC) all increased investment volume for the year and raised guidance. STAG Industrial, Inc. (STAG), Gaming and Leisure Properties, Inc. (GLPI), and VICI Properties Inc. (VICI) raised either core FFO per share or AFFO per share. First Industrial Realty Trust, Inc. (FR) narrowed the range of their guidance and maintained the midpoint of the guided FFO for 2025.

The CEO of W.P. Carey seems to have captured the prevailing sentiment in the recent earnings call.

“the market feels quite strong, constructive right now. There are lots of opportunities. I think some of this is driven by what feels like a relatively stable interest rate environment. It's probably been the case now for several quarters, which is always a key ingredient in kind of the increase in transaction activity. So that's despite changing expectations throughout the year around tariffs. If you look at treasury yields, they've been mostly range bound in the low to mid-4s for most of this year, even dating back to the fourth quarter of last year. So that creates stability. I think you see transaction activity increase…”

The key lesson that the market keeps teaching us is that it is monetary policy (interest rates) and not fiscal policy (tariffs) that drives net-lease REITs’ operating performance. With the Fed projected to cut interest rates twice this year and major institutional players like Morgan Stanley, J.P. Morgan and others introducing or expanding their net-lease fund offerings, it is not too much of a stretch to surmise that net-lease REIT share prices should be buoyant for some time.

Two specific net-lease REITs that offer investors the best balance of risk, reward, investment spread and total return are Agree Realty and Realty Income.

Let us now turn to the details of our analysis.

Investment Spreads

Please see Table 1 for the net-lease REITs that we will evaluate.

Table 1: Investment Grade, Net-lease REITs
Ticker Sector Long-term Issuer Rating Outlook Issuing Agency
ADC Free Standing BBB+ Stable S&P
EPRT Free Standing BBB- Positive S&P
FR Industrial BBB Stable S&P
GLPI Gaming BBB- Stable Fitch
NNN Free Standing BBB+ Stable S&P
O Free Standing A- Stable S&P
STAG Industrial BBB Stable Fitch
VICI Gaming BBB- Stable S&P
WPC Diversified BBB+ Stable S&P

Source: Fitch, S&P, and Nareit REITWatch

There are two salient features that inform our analysis of net-lease REITs in this article. The first is that REITs, by design, cannot retain a significant amount of earnings. They must pay at least 90% of their taxable income without regard to dividend payments in order to maintain their REIT status. This means that for the REITs to grow revenue above the modest escalation that is included in their agreements with tenants, they must access debt and equity markets for capital and deploy that capital at a rate of return above the cost of debt and equity capital.

The second feature is that in a net-lease arrangement the tenant pays for all property-related costs. This includes real estate taxes, insurance, property operating expenses and upkeep. In substance, net-lease REITs are spread investors. They access debt and equity markets for capital, then acquire real estate and lease it to tenants in a net-lease arrangement. The rental income should cover the cost of equity and debt financing. Net-lease REITs have an economic profit that is the difference between the cost of acquisition of the real estate and the rental income derived from the acquired real estate. Therefore, to measure the health of a net-lease REIT at a particular point in time, we must evaluate this spread. The spread can be measured as the difference between the capitalization rate, or cap rate, and the cost of capital. The cap rate is frequently the net operating income of the real estate asset divided by the asset value. The cost of capital is the cost of equity and debt financing in proportion to how much of each is used in the business. The wider the spread, the more economic profit is being generated by the REIT.

Let us now turn to measuring the investment spreads of our REITs.

Capitalization Rate

REITs will have different cap rates because of the idiosyncrasies of their business model. Industrial net-lease REITs will have different cap rates than free-standing retail net-lease REITs. Even within a particular sector, there may be different cap rates depending on the relative size of the net-lease REIT and their bargaining power relative to their tenants. Whatever the determining factor may be, the cap rate is a reflection of the rate of return that the REIT can achieve in the current environment. For a business that is precluded from retaining capital, the cap rate must be higher than the cost of securing equity and debt financing for the investment to be accretive.

Let us take a look at the cap rates that the REITs achieved in Q2 2025. Please see Table 2.

Table 2: Net-lease REIT Cap Rates/Cash Yields
Ticker Recent Cap Rate/Cash Yield
ADC 7.10%
EPRT 7.90%
FR 6.40%
GLPI 7.75%
NNN 7.40%
O 7.50%
STAG 7.10%
VICI 7.30%
WPC 7.50%

Source: First Industrial Realty Q2 Earnings Supplement, VICI Properties Q1 2025 Earnings Supplement, STAG Industrial Press Release for Q2 2025, Press Releases and Earnings Transcripts already referenced above.

REITs don’t always measure cap rates the same way and often use a cash yield as a proxy for the cap rate. Also, sometimes, a REIT does not engage in acquiring real estate assets in the quarter. First Industrial did not report any acquisition activity in Q2 but did report one in Q1. In this case, we take the Q1 cap rate as an approximation of the cap rates that they can achieve in today’s economic environment. Another matter is reflected in the case of VICI and GLPI. Both did not report fee simple investments recently but have loan investments that could lead to a fee simple investment down the line. Investors should bear in mind that a fee simple investment in real estate is of a different character than an investment in a loan. Both may earn a similar rate of return, but a loan investment typically has a shorter duration, which means the capital would have to be redeployed again in a relatively shorter time horizon. A fee simple investment in real estate can be leased to tenants for decades.

Cost of Debt

The cost of debt is simply the interest rate at which the REIT can borrow funds. In general, the stronger the credit of the REIT, the lower the cost of borrowing. Please see Table 3.

Table 3: Median Yield on 10-year Tenor
Ticker Long-term Issuer Rating Median Yield
ADC BBB+ 5.21%
EPRT BBB- 5.21%
FR BBB 5.21%
GLPI BBB- 5.21%
NNN BBB+ 5.21%
O A- 4.91%
STAG BBB 5.21%
VICI BBB- 5.21%
WPC BBB+ 5.21%

Source: Fidelity

Unsurprisingly, Realty Income has the lowest cost of debt. Even at 4.91%, Realty Income can probably secure borrowing at a lower actual rate because of their portfolio exposure to Europe which gives them access to the lower interest rates available in Europe. W.P. Carey also has exposure to Europe and benefits from this same dynamic. Finally, Agree Realty would probably have a lower cost of debt than what is on the table in light of their recent upgrade from Fitch. If the reader would like to make some adjustments to the figures, we would suggest considering Realty Income as having the lowest cost of debt, followed by W.P. Carey, then Agree and then everyone else at 5.21%.

Cost of Equity

Now that we have evaluated the cost of debt, let us turn to the cost of equity. For REITs to attract investor capital, they need to offer a compelling rate of return. What rate should they offer? This question does not have a straightforward answer. For our purposes, we use three inputs. We start with the current U.S. 10-year Treasury as a proxy for the risk-free rate. For REITs to attract capital, they must offer a return above the risk-free rate. We then add a risk premium to the risk-free rate. Aswath Damodaran at NYU estimates that the current equity risk premium is at 3.84%. This estimate is well within the long-term average of between 3-5%. Finally, we incorporate the beta statistic of the stock. The beta statistic is a measure of how volatile the stock is relative to the market. A stock that is more volatile will have a beta statistic above 1.0, which has the effect of increasing the return demanded by investors. A stock that has a beta statistic of less than 1.0 will have the effect of reducing the return demanded by investors because of the muted volatility relative to the market. The market is assumed to have a beta statistic of 1.0.

Please see Table 4.

Table 4: Net Lease REITs
Ticker U.S. 10-year Treasury Equity Risk Premium β Cost of Equity
ADC 4.27% 3.84% 0.55 6.39%
EPRT 4.27% 3.84% 1.09 8.46%
FR 4.27% 3.84% 1.05 8.31%
GLPI 4.27% 3.84% 0.72 7.04%
NNN 4.27% 3.84% 0.93 7.84%
O 4.27% 3.84% 0.77 7.23%
STAG 4.27% 3.84% 0.91 7.77%
VICI 4.27% 3.84% 0.71 7.00%
WPC 4.27% 3.84% 0.79 7.31%

Source: Seeking Alpha and CNBC.

Given that a REIT’s revenue stream is contractual, it shouldn’t be surprising to find that a majority of REITs trade at a lower beta than the market. Notable exceptions to this generalization are experiential REITs like EPRT whose tenants operate in the consumer discretionary sector. First Industrial also has a beta statistic above 1.0, which could be a reflection of their business model. First Industrial does engage in some speculative development i.e., developing an asset without a tenant in hand. Agree Realty does not.

In Table 5, we have the respective weights of the different forms of capital in the particular REIT's capital structure. As an example, if Realty Income were to engage in an acquisition, they would likely finance the acquisition at about a 64% to 36% split between equity and debt financing.

Table 5: Capital Structure
Ticker Weight of Debt Weight of Equity Weight of Preferred
ADC 28.3% 70.2% 1.53%
EPRT 26.7% 73.3% 0.00%
FR 26.8% 73.2% 0.00%
GLPI 34.1% 65.9% 0.00%
NNN 36.7% 63.3% 0.00%
O 35.7% 64.3% 0.00%
STAG 31.0% 69.0% 0.00%
VICI 33.1% 66.9% 0.00%
WPC 38.1% 61.9% 0.00%

Source: Seeking Alpha, Q2 2025 Earnings Supplement for EPRT, First Industrial, Realty Income, VICI, W.P. Carey, Agree Realty 10-K for 2024

In Table 6, we have our cost of acquisition or formally, our weighted average cost of capital (WACC).

Table 6: Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC)
